title: CA: Civil Construction Induction
subtitle: Entry-Level Safety and Compliance — Canada
description: Essential knowledge for civil construction workers in Canada. Covers provincial OHS legislation, WHMIS 2015, Red Seal program, TAC standards, and key safety requirements for BC, Ontario, and Alberta.

lessons.title: Canada Labour Code, Provincial Acts and MOL Inspectors
lessons.content:
## Occupational Health and Safety in Canada — Jurisdictional Framework

Canada's OHS system is unique globally: it is primarily **provincially regulated** rather than nationally harmonised. Each province and territory has its own OHS legislation, enforcement body, and inspection regime. Understanding which law applies is the first step in OHS compliance.

## Federal vs Provincial Jurisdiction

**Federal jurisdiction (Canada Labour Code, Part II):**
Applies to federally regulated workplaces:
- Banks and financial institutions
- Interprovincial and international transportation (railways, airlines, trucking crossing provincial borders)
- Telecommunications and broadcasting
- Federal government departments
- Approximately 6% of Canadian workers

**Provincial/territorial jurisdiction:**
Covers all other workplaces — approximately 94% of Canadian workers:
- Construction
- Manufacturing
- Mining
- Retail, hospitality, healthcare
- Provincial government

## Canada Labour Code — Part II (Federal)

Part II of the Canada Labour Code establishes OHS requirements for federally regulated workplaces:
- **Duties of employers**: Ensure health and safety, provide training, investigate incidents
- **Duties of employees**: Take reasonable care, comply with directions, report hazards
- **Health and Safety Committees**: Required for workplaces with 20+ employees
- **Right to refuse**: Workers may refuse dangerous work — process defined in the Code
- **Enforcement**: Labour Program (Employment and Social Development Canada — ESDC)

## Provincial OHS Legislation

| Province | Legislation | Regulator |
|---------|------------|-----------|
| Ontario | Occupational Health and Safety Act (OHSA) | Ministry of Labour, Immigration, Training and Skills Development (MLITSD) |
| British Columbia | Workers Compensation Act + OHS Regulation | WorkSafeBC |
| Alberta | Occupational Health and Safety Act 2018 | Alberta OHS |
| Quebec | Act Respecting Occupational Health and Safety (LSST) | CNESST |
| Manitoba | Workplace Safety and Health Act | Safe Work Manitoba |
| Saskatchewan | OHS Act 2020 | OHS Division |

## Ministry of Labour (MOL) Inspectors

Provincial OHS inspectors (commonly called 'MOL inspectors' in Ontario) have broad powers:

**Inspection powers:**
- Enter any workplace at any time without prior notice
- Examine equipment, materials, and processes
- Take samples or photographs
- Require production of documents and records
- Interview workers and supervisors

**Enforcement tools:**
| Tool | Effect |
|------|--------|
| **Order** | Requires specific corrective action within a timeframe |
| **Stop Work Order** | Stops specified work immediately — serious hazard |
| **Order to Comply** | Remedy a contravention of the Act |
| **Prosecution** | Charges under the provincial OHS Act — fines and imprisonment |

## Penalties — Ontario OHSA Example

| Offence | Maximum Penalty |
|---------|----------------|
| Individual | $100,000 fine + 12 months imprisonment |
| Corporation | $1,500,000 fine |

Alberta OHS (2018) introduced some of Canada's highest penalties: up to $500,000 per contravention for individuals and $1.5M for corporations.

## Internal Responsibility System (IRS)

The **Internal Responsibility System (IRS)** is the foundational philosophy of Canadian OHS:
- Everyone in the workplace — employers, supervisors, and workers — shares responsibility for OHS
- Health and safety is not just a management function — it belongs to everyone
- The IRS operates BEFORE government enforcement — internal resolution is the first step
- External enforcement (government inspectors) is the last resort when IRS fails

## Key Takeaways

- Canada: primarily provincially regulated OHS — 94% of workers under provincial laws
- Federal: Canada Labour Code Part II — banks, airlines, railways, telecom (~6% of workers)
- MOL/OHS inspectors: enter without notice, issue orders, stop work, prosecute
- Ontario OHSA: up to $1.5M corporation / $100k + 12 months individual
- IRS: everyone shares OHS responsibility — government enforcement is the last resort

questions.q: Which Canadian legislation governs OHS for workers in federally regulated industries such as banks and interprovincial airlines?
options: Ontario Occupational Health and Safety Act
questions.explanation: Canada Labour Code Part II covers OHS for federally regulated workplaces — banks, airlines, railways, and telecommunications. Approximately 6% of Canadian workers fall under federal jurisdiction. All other workers fall under provincial/territorial OHS legislation.

questions.q: What percentage of Canadian workers are covered by provincial/territorial OHS legislation rather than federal?
questions.explanation: Approximately 94% of Canadian workers fall under provincial/territorial OHS jurisdiction. Only workers in federally regulated industries (banks, airlines, railways, telecom, federal government — about 6%) are covered by the Canada Labour Code.

questions.q: What is the Internal Responsibility System (IRS) in Canadian OHS?
options: A government inspection program targeting high-risk workplaces
The philosophy that everyone in the workplace shares responsibility for health and safety — employers, supervisors, and workers
An internal audit system required by the Canada Labour Code
A workers' compensation claims management system
questions.explanation: The IRS is the foundational Canadian OHS philosophy — every workplace party (employers, supervisors, workers) shares responsibility for health and safety. Government enforcement is the backup when the internal system fails, not the primary mechanism.

questions.q: A Ministry of Labour inspector arrives at a civil construction site. Can they enter without prior notice?
options: Yes — inspectors can enter any workplace at any time without prior notice
questions.explanation: Provincial OHS inspectors (MOL inspectors) can enter any workplace at any time without prior notice. This is a fundamental enforcement power that enables effective OHS regulation — advance notice would allow hazards to be concealed.

questions.q: Under the Ontario OHSA, what is the maximum fine for a corporation convicted of an OHS offence?
questions.explanation: The maximum fine for a corporation under the Ontario OHSA is $1,500,000. Individual maximum penalties are $100,000 and/or 12 months imprisonment. These penalties were significantly increased from earlier levels to improve deterrence.

lessons.title: Right to Know, Right to Participate and Right to Refuse
lessons.content:
## The Three Fundamental Worker Rights in Canadian OHS

Canadian OHS legislation — both federal (Canada Labour Code) and provincial acts — enshrines three fundamental worker rights. These rights are the cornerstone of worker protection and are central to the Internal Responsibility System.

## Right 1 — The Right to Know

Workers have the right to know about hazards in their workplace:

**What workers have a right to know:**
- Hazardous materials they work with (WHMIS 2015 — SDSs and labels)
- Physical, biological, and chemical hazards in their work environment
- Results of health and safety inspections
- Accident and incident statistics for their workplace
- Their rights under OHS legislation

**Employer obligations to support the right to know:**
- Provide WHMIS training on all hazardous products workers may encounter
- Post required notices (OHS Act, first aid, emergency contacts) in the workplace
- Make SDSs accessible in the work area during each shift
- Conduct site inductions covering specific workplace hazards
- Provide training in a language the worker understands

## Right 2 — The Right to Participate

Workers have the right to participate in identifying and resolving health and safety issues:

**Forms of participation:**
- **Joint Health and Safety Committees (JHSCs)**: Formal committees with equal employer/worker representation
- **Health and Safety Representatives**: In smaller workplaces below JHSC threshold
- **Workplace inspections**: Worker members of JHSCs conduct inspections
- **Incident investigations**: Workers participate in post-incident reviews
- **Consultation**: Workers consulted before major changes to work processes

**Anti-reprisal protections:**
Workers who exercise their participation rights cannot be penalised:
- Cannot be dismissed, disciplined, or threatened for participating in OHS activities
- Cannot be disadvantaged for being a JHSC member or HSR
- Reprisal complaints can be filed with the provincial labour relations board or OHS authority

## Right 3 — The Right to Refuse Dangerous Work

Workers have the right to refuse work they believe is dangerous — this is one of the most powerful worker protections in Canadian OHS law.

**Grounds for refusal:**
A worker may refuse work if they have reason to believe that:
- The equipment, machine, or thing they are to use is likely to endanger them or another worker
- The physical condition of the workplace is likely to endanger them
- Any workplace equipment, process, or thing has violated the OHS Act in a way that may endanger them

**The refusal process (Ontario OHSA example):**
1. Worker reports refusal to supervisor — explains reason
2. Supervisor investigates in presence of the worker and worker representative (JHSC member)
3. If resolved: worker returns to work
4. If not resolved: worker continues to refuse — notifies Ministry of Labour (MOL)
5. MOL inspector investigates — makes binding decision
6. During refusal: employer may assign other work; may assign another worker (who must be told of the refusal)

**Limits on the right to refuse:**
The right to refuse does NOT apply when:
- The danger is a normal part of the worker's regular employment (e.g. police, firefighters, healthcare workers in specific circumstances)
- The refusal would put another person's life, health, or safety in immediate danger

## Protection from Reprisal

Workers who exercise any of the three rights are protected from reprisal:
- Dismissal, discipline, demotion, or threats for exercising OHS rights are illegal
- Reverse onus: employer must prove action was NOT reprisal
- Remedy: reinstatement, back pay, and compensation available

## Key Takeaways

- Right to Know: hazardous materials (WHMIS), workplace hazards, inspection results, rights
- Right to Participate: JHSCs, HSRs, inspections, incident investigation, consultation
- Right to Refuse: worker believes work is dangerous — structured process — MOL intervenes if unresolved
- Anti-reprisal: reverse onus on employer — cannot dismiss/discipline workers for exercising OHS rights
- These three rights are the foundation of the Internal Responsibility System

questions.q: A construction worker believes a trench they are asked to enter is dangerously unstable. They refuse the work. What must the employer do first?
options: Assign another worker to the trench without informing them of the refusal
Investigate the situation in the presence of the worker and a worker representative (JHSC member)
Contact the Ministry of Labour immediately
questions.explanation: The first step is for the supervisor to investigate in the presence of the worker and their worker representative (JHSC member or co-worker). If resolved, the worker returns. If not resolved, the Ministry of Labour is then notified. MOL involvement comes only after internal investigation fails.

questions.q: Under Canadian OHS law, what is the 'reverse onus' in reprisal cases?
options: The worker must prove the employer committed reprisal
The employer must prove that any action against the worker was NOT reprisal for exercising OHS rights
The MOL inspector bears the burden of proof
Both parties share equal burden of proof
questions.explanation: In Canadian OHS reprisal cases, the reverse onus applies — the employer must prove that any adverse action (dismissal, demotion, discipline) was NOT related to the worker exercising their OHS rights. This is stronger protection than standard employment law.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a form of worker participation in Canadian OHS?
options: Being a member of the Joint Health and Safety Committee
Participating in workplace inspections as a worker representative
Filing a WHMIS complaint with Health Canada
questions.explanation: Filing a WHMIS complaint with Health Canada is not a form of workplace OHS participation under provincial OHS Acts. The right to participate involves JHSCs, HSRs, workplace inspections, incident investigations, and consultation on work changes.

questions.q: The Right to Know in Canadian OHS includes workers' right to access:
options: WHMIS SDSs, workplace hazard information, inspection results, incident statistics, and their OHS rights
Only information about incidents they personally witnessed
Only information approved by the employer
questions.explanation: The Right to Know is broad — it includes WHMIS hazardous material information (SDSs), physical/chemical/biological workplace hazards, results of safety inspections, accident statistics for the workplace, and workers' rights under OHS legislation.

lessons.title: JHSC Composition, Meetings and Recommendations
lessons.content:
## Joint Health and Safety Committees (JHSCs)

Joint Health and Safety Committees are a cornerstone of the Internal Responsibility System in Canadian workplaces. They bring together employer and worker representatives to collaboratively identify and resolve health and safety issues before they cause injury or illness.

## When is a JHSC Required?

Requirements vary by province — Ontario OHSA example:
- Workplaces regularly employing **20 or more workers**: JHSC required
- Workplaces with 6–19 workers: Health and Safety Representative (HSR) required
- Construction projects with **20+ workers at any one time** OR project lasting more than 3 months with 20+ workers: JHSC required

**Other provinces (general guidance):**
- British Columbia: JHSC required at workplaces with 20+ workers
- Alberta: OHS Committee or OHS Representative depending on workforce size
- Federal: JHSC required for workplaces with 20+ employees (Canada Labour Code)

## JHSC Composition

JHSCs must have **equal representation** from employers and workers:
- Minimum 2 members (1 employer + 1 worker) for smaller workplaces
- Larger workplaces: typically 4–12 members — always equal employer/worker split
- **Worker members must be selected by workers** (not appointed by management)
- At least one worker member must be a **certified member** (in Ontario for workplaces with industrial/construction hazards)

**Certified Member:**
A certified JHSC member has completed approved training (Part 1 and Part 2 certification training). Certified members have additional powers — including the right to investigate critical injuries and the right to recommend work stoppages.

## JHSC Functions

| Function | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Workplace inspections** | Regular inspections of the workplace — monthly in most provinces |
| **Incident investigation** | Investigate critical injuries, fatalities, and occupational illnesses |
| **Recommendations** | Make written recommendations to employer for OHS improvements |
| **Review programs** | Review employer OHS programs, policies, and training |
| **Meetings** | Regular meetings to discuss OHS issues — minutes recorded and posted |

## JHSC Meeting Requirements

- Meetings held at least **monthly** (Ontario: at least every 3 months for lower-risk workplaces; monthly for higher-risk)
- Meeting time is paid work time — members cannot be penalised for attending
- Minutes must be kept and posted in a prominent workplace location
- Quorum: equal representation must be present
- Co-chairs: one employer co-chair + one worker co-chair (rotate chair duties)

## JHSC Recommendations

JHSCs can make written recommendations to the constructor or employer:
- **Employer must respond in writing within 21 days** (Ontario) — either agreeing to implement, disagreeing with reasons, or proposing an alternative
- If the employer rejects a recommendation, the JHSC can refer the matter to the Ministry of Labour
- JHSC recommendations are advisory — they do not have legal force by themselves
- However, ignoring JHSC recommendations is an OHS risk indicator and may attract regulator attention

## Work Stoppage Power (Certified Members — Ontario)

In Ontario, a certified worker member and certified employer member of the JHSC can jointly recommend a work stoppage when:
- A situation exists that is likely to endanger a worker
- The situation is extremely dangerous and the employer has not or cannot remedy it promptly

The employer is legally required to act on a joint work stoppage recommendation.

## Key Takeaways

- JHSC required at 20+ workers (most provinces) — equal employer/worker representation
- Worker members must be selected by workers — not appointed by management
- Core functions: inspections, incident investigation, recommendations, reviewing OHS programs
- Monthly meetings — minutes kept and posted — paid time
- Employer must respond in writing to recommendations within 21 days (Ontario)
- Certified members: additional powers including joint work stoppage recommendation

questions.q: Under the Ontario OHSA, at what workforce size is a Joint Health and Safety Committee (JHSC) required?
questions.explanation: The Ontario OHSA requires a JHSC at workplaces that regularly employ 20 or more workers. Workplaces with 6–19 workers require a Health and Safety Representative instead of a full committee.

questions.q: How must worker members of a JHSC be selected?
options: Selected by the workers they represent — not appointed by management
Chosen by the Ministry of Labour
questions.explanation: Worker members of a JHSC must be selected by the workers they represent — this ensures genuine worker representation. Management-appointed 'worker representatives' would undermine the independence and effectiveness of the committee.

questions.q: An employer receives a written JHSC recommendation to install guarding on a piece of equipment. Under the Ontario OHSA, what must the employer do?
options: Respond in writing within 21 days — agreeing, disagreeing with reasons, or proposing an alternative
File the recommendation for the next annual safety review
questions.explanation: Under the Ontario OHSA, employers must respond in writing to JHSC recommendations within 21 days — either agreeing to implement, disagreeing with reasons, or proposing an alternative. Failure to respond is itself an OHS violation.

questions.q: What additional power do certified JHSC members have that regular members do not?
options: The right to issue stop work orders unilaterally
The right to jointly recommend a work stoppage when a dangerous situation cannot be promptly remedied
The right to prosecute the employer directly
The right to issue improvement notices
questions.explanation: In Ontario, a certified worker member and certified employer member can JOINTLY recommend a work stoppage when a dangerous situation exists and the employer has not or cannot remedy it promptly. The employer is legally required to act on this joint recommendation.

lessons.title: Fall Protection, Trenching, Mobile Equipment and Overhead Hazards
lessons.content:
## Construction Safety in Canada

Construction consistently has one of the highest fatality and serious injury rates of any Canadian industry. Provincial OHS regulations and construction-specific regulations establish detailed requirements for the most hazardous construction activities.

## Fall Protection

Falls are the leading cause of construction fatalities across all Canadian provinces.

**Ontario Construction Regulation (O. Reg. 213/91) — fall protection triggers:**
- Workers must be protected from falls of **3 metres or more** on construction projects
- OR where a fall of less than 3 metres could cause injury (e.g. onto machinery, into water, or hazardous substances)

**Accepted fall protection methods (Ontario):**
1. Guardrail system
2. Travel restraint system
3. Fall restricting system
4. Fall arrest system (full-body harness + lanyard/SRL + 5,400 lb anchorage)
5. Safety net

**Working at Heights Training (Ontario — since 2015):**
- All workers using fall protection on construction sites must complete approved Working at Heights training
- Minimum 4 hours — must be from a Ministry of Labour-approved provider
- Must be renewed every 3 years

**BC OHS Regulation (Part 11):**
- Fall protection required at **3 metres** or where a lesser fall could cause serious injury

## Trenching and Excavation

Ontario Construction Regulation O. Reg. 213/91, s.226-239 governs excavation:
- Excavations **1.2 metres or deeper** require a protective system (shoring, sloping, benching) — lower trigger than US OSHA's 5-foot rule
- A **competent worker** (equivalent to OSHA's 'competent person') must supervise excavation work
- Soil must be classified before determining protective system
- Spoil must be placed at least **1.0 metre** from the edge of the excavation

**BC OHS Regulation — excavation trigger: 1.5 metres**

## Mobile Equipment Safety

Mobile equipment on construction sites creates significant struck-by and run-over risks:
- All workers must wear hi-vis clothing within 30 metres of mobile equipment operation (Ontario)
- Reverse alarms mandatory on all mobile equipment
- Spotters required for blind-spot reversing situations
- Traffic management plan required for sites with both plant and pedestrian movement
- Pre-start inspections: mandatory daily — defects reported and equipment taken out of service
- Operators must be competent — documented training or apprenticeship

**Exclusion zones:**
- No worker within the swing radius of an excavator or crane without operator communication and specific authorisation
- Zones must be marked and communicated at pre-start meetings

## Overhead Hazard — Electrical Lines

Ontario Regulation 213/91 — electrical line clearances:
| Voltage | Minimum Clearance (Ontario) |
|---------|----------------------------|
| Up to 750 V | 3 metres |
| 750 V to 150,000 V | 3 metres |
| 150,000 V to 250,000 V | 4.5 metres |
| Over 250,000 V | 6 metres |

**Before work near overhead lines:**
- Contact the electrical utility to determine voltage and request de-energisation or guarding
- Obtain written confirmation of de-energisation before work begins
- Never assume a line is de-energised — verify with the utility

## Constructor Obligations

In Ontario, the **constructor** (the entity that contracts directly with the project owner) has special obligations:
- Ensure all employers and workers on the project comply with the OHSA and regulations
- Register projects > $50,000 with the Ministry of Labour
- Post a Notice of Project at the site
- Designate a supervisor for the project

## Key Takeaways

- Fall protection required at 3 metres in most Canadian provinces (vs 6 feet/1.8m in US OSHA)
- Ontario Working at Heights training: approved provider, minimum 4 hours, renew every 3 years
- Excavation trigger: 1.2 m Ontario, 1.5 m BC — lower than US OSHA's 5-foot trigger
- Mobile equipment: hi-vis within 30m, reverse alarms, spotters, exclusion zones
- Contact the utility before any work near overhead electrical lines — get written confirmation of de-energisation

questions.q: At what fall height does Ontario's Construction Regulation require fall protection for workers?
questions.explanation: Ontario's Construction Regulation (O. Reg. 213/91) requires fall protection at 3 metres or more — and at any height where falling could cause injury (onto machinery, into water, etc.). This is a stricter trigger than US OSHA's 6-foot (1.8m) requirement.

questions.q: Under the Ontario Construction Regulation, at what excavation depth is a protective system (shoring, sloping, benching) required?
questions.explanation: Ontario requires a protective system for excavations 1.2 metres or deeper — a stricter trigger than US OSHA's 5-foot (1.5m) threshold. BC's trigger is 1.5 metres.

questions.q: Ontario's Working at Heights training must be renewed how frequently?
questions.explanation: Ontario Working at Heights training must be renewed every 3 years. It must be completed with a Ministry of Labour-approved training provider and has a minimum duration of 4 hours.

questions.q: Before any equipment operates near overhead electrical lines in Ontario, what must be obtained from the electrical utility?
options: A verbal confirmation that lines are de-energised
Written confirmation of de-energisation and line voltage information before work begins
Only the voltage of the lines
An exemption form from the Ministry of Labour
questions.explanation: Written confirmation of de-energisation must be obtained from the utility before any work begins near overhead electrical lines. Never assume de-energisation — verbal confirmation is insufficient. Always verify in writing.

lessons.title: WHMIS 2015 — GHS, SDSs, Labels and Training Requirements
lessons.content:
## WHMIS 2015 — Canada's Hazard Communication System

WHMIS (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System) 2015 is Canada's national standard for communicating chemical hazard information in workplaces. Updated in 2015 to align with the **Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals (GHS)**, it replaced WHMIS 1988.

## Key Changes — WHMIS 1988 vs WHMIS 2015

| Feature | WHMIS 1988 | WHMIS 2015 |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Pictograms | Canadian-specific hatched border symbols | GHS red diamond pictograms |
| Documents | Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS) | Safety Data Sheet (SDS) — 16 sections |
| Classification | Canadian-specific classes | GHS-aligned international classes |
| Signal words | Not used | DANGER / WARNING |

## WHMIS 2015 Hazard Classes

**Physical hazards:**
- Flammable gases, liquids, solids, aerosols
- Oxidising gases and liquids
- Gases under pressure
- Explosives and self-reactive substances
- Pyrophoric and self-heating substances

**Health hazards:**
- Acute toxicity (Category 1–5)
- Skin and eye corrosion/irritation
- Respiratory and skin sensitisation
- Germ cell mutagenicity
- Carcinogenicity
- Reproductive toxicity
- Specific target organ toxicity (single and repeated exposure)
- Aspiration hazard

**WHMIS-specific hazard class (not in GHS):**
- **Biohazardous Infectious Materials**: Microorganisms, nucleic acids, proteins causing disease

## WHMIS 2015 Labels

**Supplier labels must include:**
1. Product identifier
2. GHS pictogram(s) — in red diamond border
3. Signal word (DANGER or WARNING)
4. Hazard statements (H-statements)
5. Precautionary statements (P-statements)
6. Supplier name, address, and emergency telephone number
7. Supplemental label information as required

**Workplace labels (secondary containers):**
When transferred to a secondary container in the workplace:
- Product identifier
- Safe handling precautions
- Reference to the SDS

## Safety Data Sheets (SDSs)

Standardised 16-section format — identical across GHS-aligned countries (US HazCom 2012, WHMIS 2015, Australia):

| Section | Content |
|---------|---------|
| 1 | Identification |
| 2 | Hazard identification — most important for workers |
| 4 | First-aid measures |
| 8 | Exposure controls/PPE — critical for selecting protection |
| 9 | Physical and chemical properties |

**SDS access:**
- Must be readily accessible to workers during their shift in their work area
- Electronic access acceptable if always available without barriers
- Locked offices or supervisor-controlled access does NOT comply

## Dual Administration

WHMIS 2015 has a dual administration structure:
- **Federal government** (Health Canada): Regulates supplier labelling requirements under the Hazardous Products Act and Hazardous Products Regulations
- **Provinces/territories**: Regulate employer workplace requirements — worker education, training, workplace labels, SDS access

## Employer Training Requirements

Employers must train workers on:
1. How to read and interpret WHMIS labels (all 6 elements)
2. How to read and use SDSs (especially Sections 2, 4, and 8)
3. The specific hazards of chemicals they work with
4. Safe use, storage, handling, and disposal procedures
5. Emergency procedures for spills, inhalation, or skin/eye exposure

**When training is required:**
- Before initial exposure to a hazardous product
- When new hazardous products are introduced
- When new hazard information becomes available (updated SDS)
- After a workplace incident involving a hazardous product

## Common Civil Construction Hazardous Products

| Product | WHMIS 2015 Hazard | Key Precautions |
|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| Diesel fuel | Flammable liquid, health hazard | No ignition sources, gloves, eye protection |
| Wet concrete | Skin/eye corrosion (alkaline) | Waterproof gloves, rubber boots, eye protection |
| Bitumen/hot mix asphalt | Combustible, potential H2S, skin burn | Heat-resistant PPE, ventilation, no confined space work without testing |
| Hydraulic fluid | Skin/eye irritant | Gloves, safety glasses |

## Key Takeaways

- WHMIS 2015: GHS-aligned — replaced WHMIS 1988 Canadian-specific symbols with red diamond pictograms
- Biohazardous Infectious Materials: unique WHMIS class not in international GHS
- SDS: 16 sections — Section 2 (hazards) and Section 8 (PPE/controls) most critical
- Workplace labels on secondary containers: product ID + safe handling + reference to SDS
- Dual administration: Health Canada regulates suppliers; provinces regulate employers

questions.q: What hazard class exists in WHMIS 2015 that does NOT exist in the international GHS?
questions.explanation: Biohazardous Infectious Materials is a unique WHMIS 2015 hazard class covering microorganisms, nucleic acids, and proteins that cause disease in humans. This class does not exist in the international GHS framework.

questions.q: Under WHMIS 2015 dual administration, who regulates employer workplace requirements such as worker training and SDS access?
questions.explanation: Under WHMIS 2015's dual administration, provinces and territories regulate employer workplace requirements — including worker training, workplace labels, and SDS access. Health Canada (federal) regulates supplier labelling requirements.

questions.q: A workplace label on a secondary container must include which three elements under WHMIS 2015?
options: Product identifier, GHS pictograms, and signal word
Product identifier, safe handling precautions, and reference to the SDS
Product identifier and SDS Section 2 text only
questions.explanation: WHMIS 2015 workplace labels for secondary containers must include: (1) product identifier, (2) safe handling precautions, and (3) reference to the SDS. They do not require all supplier label elements.

questions.q: Wet concrete is a WHMIS 2015 regulated product. What is its primary health hazard?
options: Skin and eye corrosion from its highly alkaline (high pH) chemistry
questions.explanation: Wet concrete is highly alkaline (pH ~12–13). Prolonged skin contact causes progressive alkaline burns — often initially painless, leading to serious injury before workers notice. Waterproof gloves, rubber boots, and eye/face protection are required.

questions.q: When must an employer provide WHMIS 2015 training to workers?
options: Annually at a scheduled safety training day
Before initial exposure, when new products are introduced, when new hazard information is available, and after incidents involving hazardous products
questions.explanation: WHMIS 2015 training must be provided: before initial exposure to a hazardous product, when new products are introduced, when new hazard information becomes available, and after any incident involving a hazardous product. It is ongoing, not just initial.
